repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Implements a synchronous API to load and display images using kitty protocol underneath.
Cleaned up and simpler version of #31399.

close #30889

### API Implementation

1. `vim.ui.img.set(id | data, opts) -> id`
2. `vim.ui.img.get(id) -> opts | nil`
3. `vim.ui.img.del(id) -> true | false`

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
---@class vim.ui.img.PlacementOpts
---@inlinedoc
```

@@ -0,0 +1,208 @@
+local M = {}
+
+---@brief
+---
+---EXPERIMENTAL: This API may change in the future. Its semantics are not yet finalized.
+---
+---This provides a functional API for loading and displaying images in Nvim.
+---Currently supports PNG images via the Kitty graphics protocol.
+---
+---The image provider can be changed by setting `vim.ui.img.provider` to a
+---builtin name (e.g. `kitty`) or a module path string implementing
+---|vim.ui.img.Provider|.
+---
+---Examples:
+---
+---```lua
+----- Load an image from disk
+---local image_id = vim.ui.img.load("/path/to/img.png")
+---
+----- Place the image at row 5, column 10
+---local placement_id = vim.ui.img.place(image_id, { row = 5, col = 10 })
+---
+----- Update the placement position
+---vim.ui.img.place(placement_id, { row = 8, col = 12 })
+---
+----- Remove the placement
+---vim.ui.img.hide(placement_id)
+---
+----- Remove the entire image (and all its placements)
+---vim.ui.img.hide(image_id)
+---```
+
+--- An image provider implements the terminal-specific protocol for loading,
+--- placing, and hiding images.
+---
+--- Nvim includes a built-in provider for the Kitty graphics protocol.
+--- To use a custom provider, set `vim.ui.img.provider` to a module path:
+---
+--- ```lua
+--- vim.ui.img.provider = 'my.custom.provider'
+--- ```
+---@class vim.ui.img.Provider
+---@field load fun(opts: vim.ui.img.ImgOpts): integer
+---@field place fun(id: integer, opts?: vim.ui.img.PlacementOpts): integer
+---@field hide fun(id: integer, placement_id?: integer)
+
+---The name of the image provider. Builtin: `kitty`. Can also be set to
+---a module path (e.g. `my.custom.provider`) that returns a table
+---implementing |vim.ui.img.Provider|.
+---@type 'kitty'|string
+M.provider = 'kitty'
+
+---@return vim.ui.img.Provider
+local function get_provider()
+  local builtin = {
+    kitty = 'vim.ui.img._kitty',
+  }
+
+  local mod_path = builtin[M.provider] or M.provider
+  return require(mod_path)
+end
+
+---@nodoc
+---@class vim.ui.img.State
+---@field images table<integer, vim.ui.img.ImgOpts>
+---@field placements table<integer, {img_id:integer}|vim.ui.img.PlacementOpts>
+local state = {
+  images = {},
+  placements = {},
+}
+
+---Retrieves a copy of the image or placement opts based on the id.
+---@param id integer
+---@return vim.ui.img.ImgOpts|vim.ui.img.PlacementOpts|nil opts, 'image'|'placement' kind
+function M.get(id)
+  if type(state.images[id]) == 'table' then
+    return vim.deepcopy(state.images[id]), 'image'
+  end
+
+  if type(state.placements[id]) == 'table' then
+    return vim.deepcopy(state.placements[id]), 'placement'
+  end
+
+  return nil, 'image'
+end
+
+---Returns an iterator over the loaded images, mapped by id.
+---@return Iter
+function M.images()
+  return vim.iter(pairs(state.images))
+end
+
+---Returns an iterator over the active placements, mapped by id.
+---@return Iter
+function M.placements()
+  return vim.iter(pairs(state.placements))
+end
+
+---Optional image arguments:
+---@class vim.ui.img.ImgOpts
+---@field data? string data of a loaded file
+---@field filename? string path to the file (e.g. path/to/img.png)
+
+---Load an image from filename or data.
+---@param opts string|vim.ui.img.ImgOpts
+---@return integer id
+function M.load(opts)
+  vim.validate('opts', opts, { 'string', 'table' })
+
+  -- If passed a string, we assume it is the filename
+  if type(opts) == 'string' then
+    opts = { filename = opts }
+  end
+
+  local provider = get_provider()
+  local id = provider.load(opts)
+  state.images[id] = opts
+
+  return id
+end
+
+---Optional placment arguments:
+---@class vim.ui.img.PlacementOpts

comment_data.body: does the "module path" have any functional effect or is it just a name hint? i would expect the provider to just override `vim.ui.img`, we shouldn't add a new way to do that.

comment_data.body:
don't think we need the filename param. if we drop it, this becomes:

```suggestion
    local image_id = vim.ui.img.load(vim.fn.readblob('/path/to/img.png'))
```

that also avoids the need for `ImgOpts` class, which was somewhat awkward because it has both "name" and "data" but the "name" is not really serving any purpose (except as a "hint" for troubleshooting, which may be a good idea).

comment_data.body:
should be named `zindex`, like in `nvim_open_win`.

(side note: we should strongly consider if we can manage images as extmarks or floatwins. We already have "placement" concepts: extmarks and/or floating windows. But this is not a blocker since it may be backend decision, e.g. it appears that kitty is managing the placements here.)

Potentially relevant window flags:

- `focusable`  https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/fc21433167fea240e627780aa1d100656466aca7/runtime/doc/api.txt#L3885
- `hide` https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/fc21433167fea240e627780aa1d100656466aca7/runtime/doc/api.txt#L3897
- `pinned` (https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/36130)

comment_data.body:
Do we actually need both "images" and "placements" (`vim.ui.img.placements()`) or could we just have only "images"? They already share a namespace. 

An image without placement is just bytes (plus maybe a filename, but that's just a hint). Those do not need their own "ids", nor do they need to be a first-class concept in this image interface.

comment_data.body:
Not sure why we are introducing a new `place` verb/concept. We have `set_pos` in other modules, and for reference `nvim_buf_set_extmark()` returns an id for "management" of placed extmarks. See `:help dev-naming`.

Based on existing conventions, suggested api might look like:

```lua
  local placement_id = vim.ui.img.set(image_id, { row = 5, col = 10 })  -- create
  vim.ui.img.set(placement_id, { row = 8, col = 12 }) -- update (same id)
  vim.ui.img.del(placement_id)  -- remove placement
  vim.ui.img.del(image_id)      -- remove image + all placements
```

- `del()` instead of `hide()` also follows the extmark/autocmd convention
  (nvim_del_extmark, nvim_del_autocmd). "hide" implies it could be shown again, but the implementation in this PR actually deletes.

+---Implementation of neovim's image provider using kitty.
+---@class vim.ui.img._kitty: vim.ui.img.Provider
+local M = {}
+
+---@type table<integer, {img_id:integer, opts:vim.ui.img.PlacementOpts}>
+local placements = {}
+
+---Load an image into kitty terminal without displaying it.
+---@param opts vim.ui.img.ImgOpts
+---@return integer id
+function M.load(opts)
+  local util = require('vim.ui.img._util') ---@type vim.ui.img._util
+  local id = util.generate_id()
+
+  if util.is_remote() then
+    vim.validate('opts.data', opts.data, 'string', false, 'image data required when remote')
+    M._transmit_direct(id, opts.data)
+  else
+    vim.validate('opts.filename', opts.filename, 'string', false, 'image filename required')
+    M._transmit_file(id, opts.filename)
+  end
+
+  return id
+end
+
+---Place an image somewhere in neovim.
+---@param id integer image id
+---@param opts? vim.ui.img.PlacementOpts
+---@return integer placement_id
+function M.place(id, opts)
+  local util = require('vim.ui.img._util') ---@type vim.ui.img._util
+  opts = opts or {}
+
+  local img_id = id
+  local placement_id = util.generate_id()
+
+  -- If id is an existing placement, resolve the real img_id and reuse placement_id
+  if placements[id] then
+    img_id = placements[id].img_id
+    placement_id = id
+  end
+
+  placements[placement_id] = { img_id = img_id, opts = opts }
+
+  -- Cursor management sequence
+  local cursor_save = '\0277' -- Save cursor position
+  local cursor_hide = '\027[?25l' -- Hide cursor
+  local cursor_move = string.format('\027[%d;%dH', opts.row or 1, opts.col or 1) -- Move cursor
+  local cursor_restore = '\0278' -- Restore cursor position
+  local cursor_show = '\027[?25h' -- Show cursor
+
+  -- Display image control
+  ---@type table<string, string|number>
+  local control = {
+    a = 'p', -- Place/display
+    i = img_id, -- Image ID
+    p = placement_id, -- Placement ID
+    C = '1', -- Don't move cursor after image
+    q = '2', -- Suppress responses
+  }
+
+  if opts.width then
+    control.c = opts.width
+  end
+  if opts.height then
+    control.r = opts.height
+  end
+  if opts.z then
+    control.z = opts.z
+  end
+
+  -- Send complete sequence
+  util.term_send(
+    cursor_save .. cursor_hide .. cursor_move .. M._seq(control) .. cursor_restore .. cursor_show
+  )
+
+  return placement_id
+end
+
+---Hide (aka delete) an image and all placements,
+---or if the placement id is included then just that placement.
+---@param id integer
+---@param placement_id? integer
+function M.hide(id, placement_id)
+  local util = require('vim.ui.img._util') ---@type vim.ui.img._util
+
+  if placement_id then
+    placements[placement_id] = nil
+  else
+    for pid, p in pairs(placements) do
+      if p.img_id == id then
+        placements[pid] = nil
+      end
+    end
+  end
+
+  ---@type table<string, string|number>
+  local control = {
+    a = 'd', -- Delete
+    d = 'i', -- Delete image/placement
+    i = id, -- Image ID
+    q = '2', -- Suppress responses
+  }
+
+  if placement_id then
+    control.p = placement_id
+  end
+
+  util.term_send(M._seq(control))
+end
+
+---@private
+---Transmit image via file path (local)
+---@param id integer id to associate with image in kitty
+---@param filename string path to image file
+function M._transmit_file(id, filename)

comment_data.body:
@justinmk I will say that the module approach reduces the complexity of implementing the image api by pairing it down to only the fundamentals needed, while still providing helpers and cleaner abstraction to the user on top. If someone is going to implement `vim.ui.img` directly, they'd have to rewrite other methods that this way gives them for free.

Let me know if you still want me to remove in favor of just documenting that they can replace `vim.ui.img` with their own implementation (for plugin developers and 3rd party UIs), and I'll make that change.

comment_data.body:
@justinmk we need load because kitty has multiple ways to render images including taking a string of bytes passed directly in an escape code (limited) or taking a file path to an image locally available to the terminal (optimized).

If you take away the ability to specify the file path, you've limited yourself to the slower option that may also break with the size of the file.

comment_data.body:
Image represents the data. Placement represents an instance of the image put somewhere in neovim. Kitty maintains that distinction and you need to know an image vs a placement to handle each one, and this also enabled other implementations like sixel and iterm2 to cache the data (so it's not loaded each time).

Thinking out loud, we could try to hash the bytes into a string for the kitty implementation, and use that to map to the id that kitty associates with an uploaded image. Then you just need to work with placement ids, which neovim users would just think of as the ids of the shown images.

comment_data.body: I'll revise to just use the byte method per the other comments and see what you think. In terms of breaking, this "may" be less of an issue now that `nvim_ui_send` exists because if I remember correctly the long byte sequence was causing the scenario of batch writing to the tty and could get interrupted between chunks, or hang the terminal if the image was really big (waiting for all of it to get sent over the wire). For instance, if you sent `1234567` (pseudo example), the sequence might get interrupted (e.g. only `123` made it before something else showed up) or hang the terminal if you were sending a lot of data.

+---Utility functions tied to neovim's image api.
+---@class vim.ui.img._util
+---@field private _tmux_initialized boolean
+local M = {
+  _tmux_initialized = false,
+}
+
+---Check if image data is PNG format.
+---@param data string
+---@return boolean
+function M.is_png_data(data)
+  ---PNG magic number for format validation
+  local PNG_SIGNATURE = '\137PNG\r\n\26\n'
+
+  return data and data:sub(1, #PNG_SIGNATURE) == PNG_SIGNATURE
+end
+
+---Check if running in remote environment (SSH).
+---@return boolean
+function M.is_remote()
+  return vim.env.SSH_CLIENT ~= nil or vim.env.SSH_CONNECTION ~= nil
+end
+
+---Send data to terminal using nvim_ui_send, potentially wrapping to support tmux.
+---@param data string
+function M.term_send(data)
+  -- If we are running inside tmux, we need to escape the terminal sequence
+  -- to have it properly pass through
+  if vim.env.TMUX ~= nil then
+    -- If tmux hasn't been configured to allow passthrough, we need to
+    -- manually do so. Only required once
+    if not M._tmux_initialized then
+      local res = vim.system({ 'tmux', 'set', '-p', 'allow-passthrough', 'all' }):wait()
+      if res.code ~= 0 then
+        error('failed to "set -p allow-passthrough all" for tmux')
+      end
+      M._tmux_initialized = true
+    end
+
+    -- Wrap our sequence with the tmux DCS passthrough code
+    data = '\027Ptmux;\027' .. string.gsub(data, '\027', '\027\027') .. '\027\\'
+  end
+
+  vim.api.nvim_ui_send(data)
+end
+
+---Load image data from file synchronously
+---@return string data
+function M.load_image_data(file)
+  local fd, stat_err = vim.uv.fs_open(file, 'r', 0)

comment_data.body:
Soft push for something a little more structured here like what is proposed in the provider model.

Im worried about the idea of monkeypatching to provide other providers. What does this api look like if a user has a plugin that hooks the image api and the gui provides one. Which takes precedence? Should the user have to worry about it? Should the gui just hook in themselves and if a plugin overrides so be it?

I also think the simpler api exposed by the provider model is pretty compelling. Making sure that the required implementation for guis is as small as possible to meet the defacto standard of the default provider is pretty compelling from the gui perspective.

Just my two cents.

If this is kept, then there should be an option to deal with nested *Tmux*, with some argument on the nesting level. Some function like:

```lua
local function transform_nested_tmux(data)
  local rep = tonumber(vim.env.TMUX_NEST_COUNT)
  if rep == nil then
    rep = 1
  end

  data = data:gsub('\027', string.rep('\027', 2 ^ rep))
  local header, tail = '', ''
  for i = 1, rep do
    header = header .. string.rep('\027', 2 ^ (i - 1)) .. 'Ptmux;'
    tail = tail .. string.rep('\027', 2 ^ (rep - i)) .. '\\'
  end
  return header .. data .. tail
end
```

> Im worried about the idea of monkeypatching to provide other providers

We definitely should aim for a very simple interface so that "overriding" it is not a mess. I think `vim.ui.select()` has been a successful example of that.

> What does this api look like if a user has a plugin that hooks the image api and the gui provides one. Which takes precedence?

How would those be solved by a more structured "provider" concept? There can only be 1 "image provider" for a UI, right? There can only be 1 winner, and we can ensure a good UX by showing a meaningful error for unwanted situations.

Also isn't a separate "provider" concept redundant with [UI protocol](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/39434)?

comment_regular_data.body:
@justinmk in the process of trying to clean up #31399, I messed up the history. This branch is taking everything we discussed and trying to distill it down to a simplistic API that is

1. Synchronous (not complex promises)
2. Supports kitty only (no sixel or iterm2)
3. Functional api (not object-oriented like before)
4. Removes stuff that I didn't realize that neovim had (like custom iterator in favor of vim.iter)
5. Uses `img` (@justinmk did you want this to be `_img`)? The bulk of the work started as `_img`, but to be able to generate documentation I switched to `img` in the second commit. Let me know what you want to do :)

cc @gpanders @lewis6991 as you two reviewed the monster that was the last PR.

> I can't figure out how to get docs to generate.

You need to add `_img.lua` to [gen_vimdoc.lua](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/master/src/gen/gen_vimdoc.lua)

@brianhuster looks like names that have a `_` in them are excluded, so I'll wait to hear back from @justinmk if he wants to switch this from `vim.ui._img` to `vim.ui.img`. (EDIT: I went ahead and added a commit to convert to `vim.ui.img` so I could show what the documentation would look like)

https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/17d126049a25c10b8895c27eb2041a06b46ad7da/src/gen/gen_vimdoc.lua#L784-L786
@justinmk I'm seeing more folks popping up asking to implement the image api. Any chance you'll have time in the next few weeks to review this to see if this is the direction you'd like to go?

It is definitely preferable to use local transmission mediums over
sending data encoded in escape codes, when possible. That's a no brainer
from a performance and resource usage perspective.

Please delete if too off topic, but I'm quite excited for this API as I need inline images for effective note taking ( https://www.bbkane.com/blog/how-i-take-notes/ ) and I'm hoping this image API will let me replace/supplement Typora

This looks very interesting. I'm investigating what it would take to integrate this in a branch of neovide as a means to test. Would such an effort be useful at this time to verify the alternative api? or should I hold off for a bit longer to let this cook a bit?

Is there a vision for how space will be reserved within the grid for the images? I hear about unicode placeholders in the grid which seems interesting but also there probably needs to be a plan for how pixel perfect sizes can be reserved.

Theres a very similar problem with text that has multiple font sizes which can break the grid constraint as well. Maybe theres a solution that can handle both?

Edit: Reading more closely to the review comments seems like absolute positioning and no space reservation is all for this PR. Fair enough. I am very interested in the solution to that problem long term though as it seems like the "hard part" of this feature from my perspective.

Been awhile. @justinmk I ended up making a singular new commit that combined some of the earlier changes of yours I accepted as well as trying to address the other comments here.

1. Switched to set/get/del like we talked about
2. Got rid of the provider concept (someone (@Kethku ?) mentioned wanting to keep it - couldn't find the comment - but maybe with this simpler approach it doesn't matter?)
3. Removed file path loading (@lewis6991 I provided an example using the blob function, but assuming it would work just fine with `uv.fs_open` or `io.open` reading bytes as well)
4. Merged image/placement into a single concept (one ID per set() call)
5. Removed images() and placements() listing functions
6. Renamed z to zindex
7. Added @inlinedoc (from what you already recommended, @justinmk )
8. Partial updates via set(id, opts) merge with existing opts

@kovidgoyal @justinmk I removed the local file transfer. Just want to doubl-check that's what we want to do given it's less optimal in terms of performance. I guess if we wanted to add an optimization in the future, we could support passing the path instead of bytes or an id, but since the bytes are represented as a string I'm not sure how we'd check other than seeing if the "bytes" passed in resolve to a file path that exists, and if so then we'd use the local file transfer. But to make sure this works on remote machines (ssh, etc) we'd still have to detect and fall back to the direct transfer?

@justinmk I left the tmux code in for now. Would you rather I remove it?

Still not touching the relative positioning (where we'd use unicode placeholders) as I'd prefer to keep that separate and submit a followup PR that provides that feature.

If the *Tmux* code is removed or if nested *Tmux* isn't included, can you still leave a way to hook into the `term_send()` function or some other way to add support? It's just a local function now. Otherwise people who use nested *Tmux* won't be able to use the new API at all.

> If the _Tmux_ code is removed or if nested _Tmux_ isn't included, can you still leave a way to hook into the `term_send()` function or some other way to add support? It's just a local function now. Otherwise people who use nested _Tmux_ won't be able to use the new API at all.

Would it make more sense for us to remove the tmux support here and let someone override the `vim.api.nvim_ui_send()` call to do the tmux logic instead? Similar to how we're framing to replace `vim.ui.img` with your own instance. Or @stevenxxiu do you have an idea of what experience you'd expect?

I know neovim has code in places like clipboard to check for tmux and do things. @justinmk any thoughts on this one? If we add support for tmux, should we move that one-time setup logic to be a health check instead? I don't know how many people would enable passthrough mode themselves. Or how many would know to override `vim.api.nvim_ui_send()` to support the passthrough logic if in tmux.

I've been told that tmux passes through stuff it doesn't understand. It's silly that tmux requires special wrapping...

Anyway, we should think about how to solve that generally. Maybe `vim.api.nvim_ui_send()` itself should handle that.

>  let someone override the `vim.api.nvim_ui_send()` call to do the tmux logic instead

Overriding `vim.api` functions is not really a supported thing and is a bad idea. That's different than the rest of the `vim.*` Lua stdlib.

> Would it make more sense for us to remove the tmux support here

For this PR specifically it doesn't matter too much. The main thing here is to make sure we don't build-in expectations into the `vim.ui.img` interface that prevent us from changing the approach later.

I'm in favor of removing tmux support here for now, since I don't care about tmux :)

> should we move that one-time setup logic to be a health check instead? I don't know how many people would enable passthrough mode themselves

Yes that sounds like a good compromise. Really, tmux users should figure out how to convince tmux to stop getting in the way.

@justinmk here's what I've got from looking at how some of the other checks were done. I put it into a new healthcheck as I figured that this is both a new feature and also we could look in the future (if we can ever do terminal queries for it) to see if a terminal supports the kitty graphics protocol and include that as a health check as well. Only thing I'm not sure of is if being in tmux should always be an error. I check for passthrough being set like we discussed. But if someone reads this and passthrough is enabled, this still won't work (to display images) unless you wrap the terminal output in those tmux-specific escape codes. Also, I updated the simplification commit to remove the tmux code and just use the `nvim_ui_send` directly. ### Not in tmux ### In tmux, and passthrough set ### In tmux, but passthrough not set

I'm just after something that can let me transform the data so it works with nested *Tmux*. Here's some function I wrote to use with the *snacks* plugin: 

```lua
local function transform_nested_tmux(data)
  local rep = tonumber(vim.env.TMUX_NEST_COUNT)
  if rep == nil then
    rep = 1
  end

  data = data:gsub('\027', string.rep('\027', 2 ^ rep))
  local header, tail = '', ''
  for i = 1, rep do
    header = header .. string.rep('\027', 2 ^ (i - 1)) .. 'Ptmux;'
    tail = tail .. string.rep('\027', 2 ^ (rep - i)) .. '\\'
  end
  return header .. data .. tail
end
 ```
